text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- CLINICAL TRIAL FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Phase I/II KRAS G12D Inhibitor Trial -- Protocol Assessment Visit (Cycle 3)

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
TRIAL SITE: Regional Cancer Center, Medical Oncology Department

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Gerald T. Holloway is a 59-year-old male with metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, BRAF wild-type, stage IVA at diagnosis) who presents today for his protocol-specified Cycle 3 tumor assessment visit while enrolled on a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating an investigational oral KRAS G12D-selective inhibitor. He is here for safety assessment, toxicity evaluation, laboratory review, and discussion of early tumor marker response data.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Holloway is well known to this clinic. He is a 59-year-old former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit approximately 12 years ago) and civil engineer who was initially diagnosed with stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 21 months ago, presenting with a 4.8 cm sigmoid mass and synchronous hepatic metastases (4 lesions, largest 3.1 cm in segment VI, none in other organs at that time). His disease has been characterized molecularly as KRAS G12D-mutant (VAF 41% on initial NGS, confirmed at 38% VAF on re-biopsy of a pulmonary metastasis at trial entry), TP53 p.R175H co-mutation, APC p.R1450* loss-of-function mutation, MSS by both IHC and NGS, BRAF V600E wild-type, and NRAS wild-type. TMB was low at 6 mut/Mb. HER2 was not amplified. The molecular profile rendered him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) due to RAS mutant status, and ineligible for immune checkpoint inhibitors due to MSS/low-TMB status.

He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy approximately 5 weeks prior to initiating systemic therapy, with an R0 resection (pT3 pN2a, 3/18 nodes positive, LVI present, perineural invasion present). He received first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab starting approximately 20 months ago. He achieved a meaningful partial response after 6 cycles (hepatic metastases reduced by approximately 39-44% across lesions), and continued through cycle 10, at which point dose-limiting cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy (grade 2-3) necessitated discontinuation of oxaliplatin. He transitioned to maintenance 5-fluorouracil plus bevacizumab. Disease remained controlled through approximately 10 months from initial treatment start, at which point restaging imaging demonstrated hepatic lesion regrowth and two new pulmonary nodules in the right lung (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.5 cm), with a concurrent rise in CEA from 22 to 41 ng/mL and CA 19-9 from 19 to 34 U/mL. This was consistent with radiographic progression.

He was transitioned to second-line therapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab approximately 10.5 months ago. He achieved a partial response after 6 cycles, with hepatic and pulmonary lesion shrinkage and CEA declining to 22 ng/mL nadir. The regimen was complicated by grade 3 hypertension around cycles 8-9, managed with a brief ramucirumab hold and intensification of his antihypertensive regimen (amlodipine dose increased to 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg added). After 12 cycles of second-line therapy, disease was stable. However, restaging imaging after cycle 18 (approximately 19 months from initial diagnosis) demonstrated hepatic lesion regrowth (segment VI 1.5 cm, IVb 1.2 cm, II 0.6 cm, VII 0.4 cm), pulmonary nodule growth (right lower lobe 0.9 cm), and rising tumor markers (CEA 58 ng/mL, CA 19-9 47 U/mL). At that time, ECOG performance status had declined slightly to 1-2, with increased cumulative fatigue.

Given confirmed KRAS G12D driver mutation at progression (re-biopsy of right lower lobe pulmonary nodule confirmed metastatic CRC with KRAS G12D VAF 38%, no new acquired resistance mutations identified), he was referred to this tertiary cancer center and enrolled on the current Phase I/II investigational trial approximately 6 weeks ago. His most recent CT restaging prior to trial enrollment confirmed multifocal hepatic metastases and bilateral (predominantly right-sided) pulmonary nodules as the measurable sites of disease. A fresh tumor biopsy was obtained per protocol at enrollment.

Today marks his cycle 3 assessment. He has received two full cycles of the oral investigational KRAS G12D-selective inhibitor. He reports overall tolerability has been better than expected. He has noticed gradual improvement in energy over the past few weeks and is cautiously optimistic about his progress, particularly given the improvement in his tumor markers from the time of trial enrollment.

With respect to specific symptoms today, he reports:
- Mild persistent nausea, primarily in the mornings and approximately 1-2 hours after taking the investigational agent; this has been manageable without significant dietary restriction. He rates this a 2-3/10 in severity. Ondansetron taken as needed with partial relief.
- A mild acneiform rash noted on his face and upper trunk. He was started on topical clindamycin gel at his last visit and reports modest improvement in the rash over the past 2 weeks. No pustular or crusting lesions, no involvement of mucous membranes, no secondary infection noted.
- Persistent low-grade fatigue, which he describes as somewhat improved compared to the end of his second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab course but still present. He rates fatigue a 3/10.
- His chronic residual peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin has continued to improve incrementally. He reports numbness and mild tingling predominantly in the soles of his feet, with some persistence of reduced fine sensation in his fingertips, but no functional impairment at this time. He can button shirts, handle fine objects, and ambulate on even and moderately uneven surfaces without difficulty.
- No new or worsening diarrhea (he notes he had significant diarrhea on irinotecan, which has resolved since stopping FOLFIRI; current stool frequency is 1-2 normal formed stools per day).
- No significant mucositis, dysphagia, or oral discomfort.
- No chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath.
- Blood pressure has been self-monitored at home. Readings over the past 2 weeks: ranging from 130-142/82-88 mmHg. He continues on his triple antihypertensive regimen without any recent adjustments.
- No edema in the lower extremities.
- No headaches, visual changes, or neurologic symptoms beyond the chronic neuropathy.
- Appetite remains diminished relative to his pre-illness baseline but is adequate to maintain weight (he reports eating 2-3 meals per day, primarily soft foods).
- Weight: he reports subjective stability in weight over the past month.
- No abdominal pain, distension, or rectal bleeding since his colectomy.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Fatigue as above (grade 1, improving). No fever, chills, or night sweats. Weight subjectively stable.
Head/Eyes/ENT: No headache, vision change, tinnitus, epistaxis, or oral ulcers.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or exertional dyspnea. Blood pressure managed on triple antihypertensive therapy.
Respiratory: No new cough, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, or dyspnea at rest. Pulmonary nodules under surveillance.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent nausea. No vomiting, abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or diarrhea. Normal bowel function post-colectomy.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or frequency.
Musculoskeletal: No new arthralgias or myalgias. Bilateral foot numbness as noted (residual neuropathy).
Neurologic: Residual grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in feet and mild fingertip hypoesthesia. No motor weakness, balance difficulty, or new symptoms.
Dermatologic: Mild acneiform rash on face and upper chest, improving on topical clindamycin. No nail changes, hand-foot syndrome (expected to be absent with current regimen, as this was a concern with prior 5-FU -- no capecitabine used).
Psychosocial: Patient reports cautious optimism given marker response. Some anxiety about disease trajectory. No depression reported. Supported by wife and adult children.
Endocrine: No symptoms of adrenal insufficiency.

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Investigational oral KRAS G12D inhibitor -- dose per trial protocol (assigned dose level), taken orally once daily with food
2. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
3. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
4. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
5. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily (hyperlipidemia)
6. Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed every 8 hours for nausea (up to 3 times daily)
7. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (backup antiemetic)
8. Topical clindamycin 1% gel applied to face and upper chest twice daily (investigational agent-related acneiform rash)
9. Loperamide 2 mg as needed for diarrhea (not recently required; kept on hand)
10. Multivitamin daily

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies. No prior reactions to iodinated contrast (received multiple contrast-enhanced CTs without issue).

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (summarized for completeness):
- Stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS, BRAF WT), currently under trial treatment -- see HPI
- Hypertension: predating cancer diagnosis, previously well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg; regimen intensified progressively due to bevacizumab and then ramucirumab-related exacerbation; now on triple therapy
- Hyperlipidemia: on statin therapy, stable
- Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy (grade 2-3 at nadir, improved to grade 1 currently)
- Laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis (approximately 21 months ago), R0, uncomplicated recovery

FAMILY HISTORY: No personal or family history of colorectal cancer; no family history of Lynch syndrome or hereditary polyposis syndromes.

SOCIAL HISTORY: 59-year-old male, works as civil engineer (currently on reduced hours due to treatment demands). Former smoker, 20 pack-years, quit 12 years ago. Occasional alcohol use (reports approximately 1 drink per week since diagnosis, down from prior social drinking). Married, lives with spouse. Has adult children who are engaged and supportive. No illicit drug use. No significant occupational or environmental exposures beyond remote tobacco use.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-nourished, well-groomed male appearing stated age. No acute distress. Conversational, engaged in discussion of his results.
Vitals: Blood pressure 135/84 mmHg (left arm, seated), heart rate 74 bpm (regular), temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 96% on room air, respiratory rate 16/min, weight 81.2 kg (baseline at diagnosis approximately 84 kg, down approximately 3 kg over 21 months), height 176 cm, BMI 26.2 kg/m2.
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out light work and sedentary activities).
Head/Eyes/ENT: Normocephalic. Sclerae anicteric. No oral mucositis; mild geographic tongue noted (possibly related to prior 5-FU). Moist mucous membranes. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.
Skin/Dermatologic: Grade 1 acneiform follicular eruption distributed on bilateral cheeks, chin, and upper chest wall. Papular lesions without significant pustulation. No crusting, weeping, or signs of secondary bacterial superinfection. No jaundice. No palmar erythema or spider angiomata. No hand-foot syndrome. Mild brittle changes in fingernails bilaterally, likely chronic chemotherapy effect.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles. Good air movement throughout.
Abdominal: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Midline infraumbilical laparoscopic port-site scars well-healed. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No palpable abdominal masses. Normoactive bowel sounds. No ascites.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No calf tenderness or swelling (DVT precaution examined given prior anti-angiogenic therapy history).
Neurologic: Alert, oriented, fluent. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Gait: normal cadence on flat surface, mild hesitancy noted when asked to walk in tandem (consistent with residual neuropathy in feet, no frank ataxia). Sensory: reduced pinprick sensation in bilateral feet (worse distally); fingertip light touch slightly reduced bilaterally but markedly improved from prior documented neuropathy peak. Romberg: mildly positive (slight sway).
Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

LABORATORY RESULTS (today's visit):
Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.2 x10^9/L (reference 4.0-11.0)
- Absolute neutrophil count: 2,950/uL (adequate)
- Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL (reference 13.5-17.5; mild anemia, consistent with chronic disease/prior chemotherapy effect; improved from nadir of 10.8 g/dL on FOLFIRI)
- Platelets: 198 x10^9/L (normal)
- MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L, Chloride: 104 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 25 mEq/L
- BUN: 16 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2; baseline creatinine approximately 0.9 mg/dL)
- Glucose: 95 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT: 44 U/L (mildly elevated above institutional ULN of 40; improved from 52 at prior visit)
- AST: 41 U/L (mildly elevated; improved from 48 at prior visit)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 98 U/L (normal; consistent with hepatic metastatic disease not progressing)
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL, Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
- LDH: 212 U/L (mildly elevated above ULN; chronic pattern consistent with tumor burden)

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 42 ng/mL (at trial enrollment approximately 6 weeks ago: 58 ng/mL; at peak on prior regimen: 58 ng/mL; showing a decline of approximately 28% from enrollment)
- CA 19-9: 35 U/mL (at trial enrollment: 47 U/mL; declining; also improved approximately 26% from enrollment baseline)

Coagulation (per protocol): PT/INR 1.1, PTT 28 seconds -- normal.
Thyroid function (per protocol, cycle 3 assessment): TSH 2.3 mIU/L -- normal.
Lipase (per protocol): 42 U/L -- normal.

IMAGING RESULTS:
No new imaging obtained today (this is a between-scan safety assessment visit; formal restaging CT is scheduled at cycle 6 per protocol). Tumor marker trending noted above. The most recent CT (from prior to trial enrollment, approximately 6 weeks ago at progression on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab) demonstrated: segment VI hepatic metastasis 1.5 cm, segment IVb 1.2 cm, segment II 0.6 cm, segment VII 0.4 cm, right lower lobe pulmonary nodule 0.9 cm (biopsy-confirmed metastatic CRC), right upper lobe pulmonary nodule 0.5 cm.

ASSESSMENT:
1. Metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, BRAF wild-type, TP53 co-mutant. Stage IVA at initial diagnosis (pT3 pN2a M1a), now with hepatic and pulmonary metastases. Currently enrolled on Phase I/II clinical trial with oral investigational KRAS G12D-selective inhibitor. This is cycle 3; patient has completed two full treatment cycles.

Early response assessment based on tumor marker kinetics is encouraging -- CEA has declined from 58 to 42 ng/mL (approximately 28% decrease) and CA 19-9 from 47 to 35 U/mL (approximately 26% decrease) since initiating the investigational agent. While marker trends are not definitive, they are consistent with early biological activity of the KRAS G12D inhibitor. Formal radiographic assessment is pending at cycle 6 (next scheduled restaging CT in approximately 6 weeks).

The patient's disease has now progressed through two prior lines of standard-of-care chemotherapy (mFOLFOX6/bevacizumab and FOLFIRI/ramucirumab), and he is correctly enrolled on this trial as third-line therapy, given his confirmed KRAS G12D driver mutation and absence of secondary resistance alterations on re-biopsy. His prior therapeutic course has been notable for significant oxaliplatin-related cumulative neuropathy (persisting at grade 1) and bevacizumab/ramucirumab-exacerbated hypertension now managed on triple therapy.

2. Investigational agent-related toxicities -- manageable:
- Grade 1 nausea: being managed with as-needed ondansetron and prochlorperazine. No dose modification required at this time. Patient should be counseled to take the investigational agent consistently with a meal to potentially reduce GI side effects. If nausea escalates to grade 2 or interferes significantly with oral intake or adherence, dose modification considerations will be discussed per protocol.
- Grade 1 acneiform rash (follicular eruption, face and upper chest): this pattern of rash has been seen with various agents targeting the RAS-MAPK signaling axis, and may be a pharmacodynamic marker of on-target activity, though this is speculative for this investigational compound. Topical clindamycin has been initiated and appears to be providing modest benefit. Patient instructed to use gentle, non-comedogenic moisturizer and avoid irritating topical agents. Dermatology referral to be placed if rash escalates to grade 2 (more widespread, pustular, or painful) or fails to respond to current management.
- Grade 1 fatigue: expected; improving compared to end of prior FOLFIRI/ramucirumab course. No intervention indicated beyond supportive counseling, maintenance of light physical activity as tolerated, and adequate nutrition.

3. Residual oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 1 (improved from grade 2-3 nadir): Stable to slightly improving. The current investigational agent (oral KRAS G12D inhibitor) is not expected to contribute to peripheral neuropathy. This continues to improve incrementally and is not functionally limiting at this time. No intervention planned; continued monitoring.

4. Hypertension, currently controlled on triple antihypertensive regimen (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg): Blood pressure today 135/84 mmHg. This remains well-controlled on current regimen. The investigational KRAS G12D inhibitor is not known to have significant anti-angiogenic properties; hypertension management reflects primarily carryover effect from prior bevacizumab and ramucirumab exposure. Blood pressure to be checked at each visit per standard of care; no medication adjustments required today.

5. Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.2 g/dL): Likely anemia of chronic disease and residual myelosuppression from prior platinum-based and irinotecan-based chemotherapy. No active bleeding. Improved from prior low of 10.8 g/dL. Iron studies not recently checked -- will add to next labs. No transfusion threshold reached; no erythropoiesis-stimulating agent planned at this time.

6. Mildly elevated transaminases (ALT 44 U/L, AST 41 U/L): Mildly above institutional upper limits of normal but stable and improving compared to prior visit. Most likely related to hepatic metastatic disease or residual effect of prior chemotherapy-related hepatotoxicity. No clinical pattern of drug-induced liver injury. Will monitor at next visit; if transaminases rise to greater than 3x ULN, protocol-specified hepatotoxicity assessment will be initiated.

PLAN:
1. Continue oral investigational KRAS G12D inhibitor at current protocol-assigned dose level. Patient confirmed understanding of dosing schedule, storage requirements, and when to call the trial nurse line.
2. Cycle 3 treatment continues per protocol; next protocol-specified visit in 2 weeks for cycle 4 safety check.
3. Formal restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast is scheduled at cycle 6 (approximately 6 weeks from today) per trial protocol. This will be the first formal radiographic assessment on the investigational agent.
4. Repeat labs at next visit including CBC, CMP, LFTs, LDH, CEA, CA 19-9, and protocol-specified safety labs.
5. Add iron studies to next lab draw to further evaluate the mild normocytic anemia.
6. Continue current antihypertensive regimen unchanged; home blood pressure monitoring to continue.
7. Topical clindamycin continued for acneiform rash; patient educated on skin care. Dermatology referral to be placed if rash worsens.
8. Ongoing oncology support: patient encouraged to contact the trial nurse line or on-call oncologist with any new or worsening symptoms, especially any grade 3 toxicity, fever, signs of infection, or significant GI events.
9. Palliative care integration: deferred for now given stable functional status and patient's expressed desire to pursue active treatment. Supportive care resources reviewed with patient and spouse; social work available as needed.
10. Goals of care: Patient expresses clear desire to continue active oncologic treatment as long as reasonable therapeutic options and acceptable quality of life are maintained. Family is supportive and informed. Full code status confirmed. Will reassess prognosis and goals of care at cycle 6 restaging assessment once formal radiographic data is available.

Patient seen, examined, and discussed in detail with the primary oncologist. Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and expressed agreement. Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist, MD], Medical Oncology / Clinical Trials
[Regional Cancer Center]
